trajectory_summary: This is a recycled-lead callback where the customer arrived with a specific, narrow ask (battery only, not solar) and a scheduling agenda. Carter successfully navigated genuine scheduling friction and landed a Saturday 11AM appointment — that's a real outcome. However, the call is structurally fragile: no orphaned-flow qualification was run on the existing solar system, no button-up protocol elements were delivered, and the product mismatch from the prior touch ('Delta solar panel') was left unaddressed. The closer will walk into an appointment with an existing-solar customer whose specific need (battery) has not been qualified against Ion's actual offering (Tesla Powerwall), and whose commitment to the appointment rests on a text confirmation alone with no verbal handshake.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, so 'unknown' as a v1 tag understates the outcome. However, the appointment is conditionally fragile — no button-up protocol, no orphaned-flow qualification, and an unresolved product-name artifact from the prior touch. The booking is real but the sit probability is lower than a fully buttoned-up call would produce.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Orphaned-flow qualification on battery-only / existing-solar customers
primary_coaching_focus.why: Carter accepted the battery-only framing and booked the appointment without running a single orphaned-flow question. The closer will arrive at a Saturday appointment with an existing-solar customer whose system has not been qualified (own vs. lease, installer, size, working status) and whose specific need (battery) has not been matched against Ion's actual offering (Tesla Powerwall only). This is the highest-leverage gap on this call — it's not a persuasion failure, it's a qualification failure that puts the closer in a blind spot and risks a wasted sit.

key_moments.quote: No. The thing is the person said that, you guys have a Delta solar panel for me to take a look at. But I was saying I already have solar panel. All I needed was, like, batteries.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer is flagging two things simultaneously: (1) a product-name artifact from the prior touch ('Delta solar panel') that doesn't match Ion's catalog, and (2) their actual need — battery only, existing solar system already in place. This is the orphaned-flow trigger. Carter's response ('Just a battery? Okay') acknowledges the need but skips the entire qualification sequence: own vs. lease, original installer, system size, working status. On a recycled lead, the prior touch may have captured some of this — but Carter doesn't reference any prior data, and the closer will have no qualification context. Strategic read: Carter may be deferring qualification to the closer given this is a rebook. Reactive read: Carter simply didn't recognize the orphaned-flow trigger. The reactive read is more supported — there's no evidence of deliberate deferral, and the 'Just a battery? Okay' response reads as acceptance, not strategy.

key_moments.quote: you guys have a Delta solar panel for me to take a look at
key_moments.stewart_read: 'Delta solar panel' is an unresolved product-name artifact from the prior touch. It doesn't match Ion's catalog (Ion sells its own panels, not a 'Delta' brand). Carter does not clarify what this refers to, does not correct the customer's framing, and does not establish what Ion actually offers. The customer may arrive at the closer appointment expecting a product Ion doesn't carry. This is a narrower version of the tesla_expectation_gap pattern — not Tesla roof tiles, but a named product from a prior rep's framing that needs to be resolved before the closer call.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We can reschedule that for you. I think let me look at yeah. It says it's just for a battery. That's just a generic text that goes out.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter's transparency about the generic text ('That's just a generic text that goes out') is a small trust-building move — it explains the product mismatch the customer flagged without being defensive. This is a micro-trade: Carter substitutes a process explanation for a personal commitment, which reduces the customer's mild corrective friction. The move works — the customer doesn't push back further on the mismatch. However, it's a floor-level trust move, not a ceiling-level one. Carter could have followed it with a direct acknowledgment of the customer's actual need to deepen the trust signal.

key_moments.quote: Okay. What about gonna be in July next week. I'll be back on Tuesday.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer is actively problem-solving the scheduling — they're not looking for an exit, they're looking for a time that works. 'I'll be back on Tuesday' is a buying signal: this customer wants the appointment. Carter correctly reads this and pivots to Tuesday, then Wednesday when Tuesday proves difficult. The signal is handled adequately. What Carter misses is the opportunity to anchor the customer's motivation at this moment — the customer is clearly invested enough to work around a tight schedule, which is the emotional hook for the closer. Carter never asks why the battery matters to them, which would have given the closer a value anchor.
